question: What is an assumable mortgage?
answer: An assumable mortgage lets a buyer take over the seller's existing loan — same rate, same terms, same remaining balance. If the seller locked in a 2.75% rate in 2021, you get that 2.75% rate. With today's market rates around 7%, that's a massive savings. Not all loans are assumable — FHA, VA, and USDA loans are. Conventional loans generally are not.

question: How do I assume a mortgage?
answer: Here's the process: 1) Find a home with an assumable loan (that's what we do — we track every one in Colorado). 2) Make an offer and get it accepted. 3) Contact the seller's lender and request an assumption package. 4) Submit your financials — they'll qualify you similar to a new loan. 5) Close the assumption. The whole process typically takes 60-120 days because lenders aren't staffed for it. We guide you through every step.

question: What's the equity gap?
answer: The equity gap is the difference between the home's purchase price and the remaining loan balance you're assuming. For example: if the home is $450K and the assumable loan balance is $350K, the equity gap is $100K. You need to cover that gap — either with cash, a second lien (like a HELOC or piggyback loan), or seller financing. We help buyers strategize the best way to bridge it.

question: What rates are available on assumable mortgages?
answer: We're seeing assumable rates ranging from 2.25% to 4.5% across our Colorado listings. The sweet spot is 2.75%-3.5% — loans originated in 2020-2022 when rates were at historic lows. Compare that to today's 30-year fixed around 6.8-7.1%. On a $400K loan, a 3% assumable rate saves you roughly $900/month compared to a 7% market rate. That's over $10,000/year in your pocket.

question: What's the difference between VA and FHA assumptions?
answer: Both VA and FHA loans are assumable, but there's a key difference: FHA assumptions — anyone can assume an FHA loan. You don't need to be a first-time buyer or meet FHA-specific requirements beyond qualifying with the lender. VA assumptions — anyone can assume a VA loan too, BUT if the buyer isn't a veteran, the seller's VA entitlement stays tied up until the loan is paid off. That's why many VA sellers prefer veteran buyers. We help navigate both types.

question: How long does a mortgage assumption take?
answer: Honestly? Longer than a traditional purchase. Plan for 60-120 days from accepted offer to closing. The bottleneck is the lender — most servicers (like Mr. Cooper, Pennymac, etc.) have small assumption departments and huge backlogs. We've seen some close in 45 days and others stretch to 5 months. The savings are worth the wait — buyers saving $800-1,200/month don't mind waiting an extra month or two.

question: Can I finance the equity gap?
answer: Yes, there are several options: 1) Cash — simplest if you have it. 2) Second lien / piggyback loan — some lenders will do an 80/10/10 or similar structure. 3) HELOC on another property you own. 4) Seller financing — the seller carries a note for part of the gap. 5) Gift funds from family. The right strategy depends on your financial situation. We connect buyers with lenders experienced in assumption gap financing.

question: What markets do you serve?
answer: Right now we're focused on Colorado — we track over 800 assumable listings across the state, with heavy coverage in Colorado Springs, Denver metro, Fort Collins, Pueblo, and the mountain communities. Colorado is one of the best states for assumable deals because of the high concentration of VA loans (military bases) and FHA loans. We're expanding to other states soon.

question: How do I get started?
answer: Easy — tell me your budget range and where you're looking in Colorado, and I'll have Ryan send you matching assumable listings within the hour. No obligation, no pressure. Ryan personally reviews every match to make sure the numbers work before sending them your way. You can also browse all current listings at assumableguy.com/homes.

question: What's the full process from start to finish?
answer: Here's the full flow: 1) We match you with assumable homes that fit your budget and location. 2) You tour homes and make an offer (we help with strategy). 3) Offer accepted — we initiate the assumption with the lender. 4) You submit financials and get qualified by the lender. 5) Lender processes the assumption (60-90 day window). 6) Closing day — you take over the seller's loan at their locked-in rate. Throughout, we handle the complexity so you just focus on finding the right home.

question: What do I need to qualify for an assumption?
answer: You'll need to qualify with the seller's existing lender, similar to a traditional mortgage. Generally: credit score of 620+ for FHA (580 minimum), 620+ for VA. Stable income and employment history. Debt-to-income ratio under 43-50% depending on the lender. The good news: you DON'T need a new appraisal in most cases, which saves time and money.

question: How much can I save with an assumable mortgage?
answer: The savings are massive right now. On a $400K loan: at 7% market rate you'd pay $2,661/month. At 3% assumable rate you'd pay $1,686/month. That's $975/month savings — $11,700/year — $351,000 over the life of the loan. Even if you pay a slight premium on the purchase price, the interest savings usually dwarf it within the first few years.

question: What are the costs of assuming a mortgage?
answer: Assumption costs are typically lower than a traditional purchase: 1) Assumption fee from the lender — usually $500-$1,500. 2) Title insurance and closing costs — similar to a regular purchase. 3) No new appraisal fee in most cases. 4) No loan origination fee (you're not getting a new loan). Overall, you're saving on several line items compared to originating a brand new mortgage.

question: Who is The Assumable Guy?
answer: That's Ryan Thomson — a Colorado real estate agent who specializes exclusively in assumable mortgages. While most agents have never done an assumption, Ryan has built an entire system around them: tracking every assumable listing in Colorado, guiding buyers through the assumption process, and connecting them with lenders who know how to handle these deals. His team includes Abby (operations) and Bryon (agent partner).

question: Can I assume a conventional mortgage?
answer: Generally no. Most conventional loans have a 'due on sale' clause that prevents assumption. The assumable loan types are: FHA loans (Federal Housing Administration), VA loans (Veterans Affairs), and USDA loans. That said, these make up a huge portion of the market — especially in Colorado with its military communities. We focus exclusively on these assumable loan types.

question: What if I'm a seller with an assumable loan?
answer: Having an assumable loan is a huge selling advantage right now. Your low rate is a magnet for buyers — it can help you sell faster and potentially at a premium. We market your assumable rate front and center to attract qualified buyers. If you have a VA or FHA loan under 4.5%, you're sitting on a goldmine. Visit assumableguy.com/sell to learn more.

question: Can investors use assumable mortgages?
answer: Absolutely — and it's one of the best-kept secrets in real estate investing right now. Imagine picking up a rental property with a 3% mortgage when your competitors are financing at 7%. Your cash flow is dramatically better from day one. FHA assumptions work for investors, and VA assumptions work if you're a veteran. We have a dedicated deal pipeline for investor-grade assumable properties in Colorado.

question: Where can I see available assumable homes?
answer: Head to assumableguy.com/homes — we track over 800 assumable listings across Colorado, updated regularly. You can browse by city, price, and loan type. Each listing shows the assumable rate, remaining balance, and estimated monthly payment. Want personalized matches? Just tell me your budget and preferred area, and Ryan will send you the top picks.

question: Can I refinance after assuming a mortgage?
answer: Yes — once you assume the loan, it's yours, and you can refinance it later just like any other mortgage. But here's the thing: why would you? If you assumed a 3% rate, you'd only refinance if rates drop below that (unlikely anytime soon). The whole point is locking in that low rate for the long haul. Some buyers do cash-out refis later to tap equity, but that's a different strategy.

question: Is this good for first-time buyers?
answer: It's actually one of the best strategies for first-time buyers right now. Here's why: 1) Lower monthly payments mean easier qualification. 2) FHA assumptions have lower down payment requirements. 3) You skip some closing costs. 4) You lock in a rate that may not exist again for years. The main challenge is the equity gap, but we help first-time buyers strategize — including down payment assistance programs that can be combined with assumptions.
